title: Partial — flexible update operations
instruction:
When updating a record, you rarely change every field. `Partial<T>` makes all fields optional — perfect for PATCH/update operations. It's one of the most-used utility types in real codebases.

Build a user settings update function.
successMessage: `Partial<UserSettings>` only requires the fields you're changing — the rest come from the current settings via spread. This is the PATCH pattern that REST APIs use constantly.

title: Pick and Omit — shape data for APIs
instruction:
`Pick<T, K>` keeps only the fields you list. `Omit<T, K>` keeps everything except what you list. Both create new types from existing ones — no duplication.

Use them to create safe API response types that don't leak sensitive data.
successMessage: `Omit<User, "passwordHash">` creates a type that literally cannot contain `passwordHash`. If `toPublicUser` accidentally included it, TypeScript would refuse to compile. Type safety as a security layer.

title: Record — typed dictionaries and lookup maps
instruction:
`Record<K, V>` creates an object type where all keys are of type `K` and all values are of type `V`. It's cleaner than index signatures and integrates perfectly with union type keys.

Build a role-based permissions system.
successMessage: `Record<Role, Permission[]>` guarantees EVERY role in the union has a permissions entry. Add a new role to the union, and TypeScript immediately tells you to add its permissions. Zero risk of a missing case.

title: ReturnType and Parameters — derive types from functions
instruction:
`ReturnType<T>` and `Parameters<T>` extract types from existing functions. This keeps types in sync automatically — change the function signature and the derived types update everywhere.

This is how large codebases avoid type drift between function signatures and type definitions.
expectedOutput: Creating order: 3x TypeScript Pro for customer 42
successMessage: `ReturnType<typeof createOrder>` automatically matches whatever `createOrder` actually returns — no manual type duplication. If the function changes, the derived type updates automatically.
